sections.section: SCOPE
sections.summary: Recommends proper packaging and transport practices for fresh fruit and vegetables to maintain produce quality during transportation and marketing.

sections.section: SECTION II
sections.summary: Provides guidance on design, condition and loading methods for transport equipment for fresh produce, listing factors to consider when selecting mode and equipment (destination, value, perishability, quantity, temperature/humidity requirements, ambient conditions, transit time, freight rates, service quality) and recommending refrigerated road or air containers, airport temperature-controlled storage and coordination, and consideration of monitoring/display systems for temperature and relative humidity.

sections.section: 2.1 Mode of transportation and type of equipment
sections.summary: Lists factors to consider when choosing mode of transportation and type of equipment for fresh produce, including destination, value, perishability, quantity, recommended storage temperature and relative humidity, ambient temperatures, transit time, freight rates, and quality of transportation service.

sections.section: 2.2 Reliability and quality of transportation service provided by different carriers must be carefully considered  along  with  the  rates  charged
sections.summary: Advises shippers to consider carrier reliability and service quality along with rates, noting services and schedules may change weekly or be withdrawn and recommending contact with origin/destination port authorities and local trade publications for current information.

sections.section: 2.3 When available refrigerated trailers and van containers are recommended for most high volume produce with transit and storage lives of a week or more. After transit, there must be enough remaining produce  life  for  marketing.  Carriers  utilizing  trailers  and  containers  can  offer  a  door-to-door  service.  This reduces handling, exposure, damage, and theft of the produce.
sections.summary: Recommends refrigerated trailers and van containers for most high-volume produce with transit and storage lives of a week or more, requiring sufficient remaining produce life after transit and noting that door-to-door service from trailers/containers reduces handling, exposure, damage, and theft.

sections.section: 2.4 Air cargo containers also can be used to provide a door-to-door service. Produce transported by air is  generally high-value and highly perishable. Freight costs are higher by air. Transit times, however, are in terms of hours instead of days.
sections.summary: States that air cargo containers can provide door-to-door service and that produce transported by air is generally high-value and highly perishable, with higher freight costs but much shorter transit times.

sections.section: 2.5 Many produce is shipped in unrefrigerated air containers or on air cargo pallets. This requires close  coordination  at  the  origin  and  destination  airports  to  protect  the  produce  when  flights  are  delayed. Temperature-controlled  storage  facilities  at  airports  are  needed  to  ensure  produce  quality.  Refrigerated  air containers are available and should be used when possible. Use of insulated thermal blankets is an option.
sections.summary: Notes that many consignments travel in unrefrigerated air containers or on pallets, requiring close coordination at origin and destination airports and temperature-controlled airport storage to protect produce when flights are delayed, and recommends refrigerated air containers or insulated thermal blankets when possible.

sections.section: 2.6 Produce which can be shipped in refrigerated trailers and van containers are sometimes shipped by air to take advantage of brief market opportunities, such as the beginning of a season when prices are high and supply is limited. A robust and accurate system for monitoring or displaying temperature and relative humidity during transport in integral containers needs to be considered.
sections.summary: Acknowledges that produce normally carried in refrigerated trailers may be shipped by air for short market opportunities and advises consideration of robust and accurate temperature and relative-humidity monitoring or display systems for integral containers.

sections.section: Amended 2004.
sections.summary: Editorial note indicating the document was amended in 2004 (not substantive regulatory content).

sections.section: CODE OF PRACTICE FOR
sections.summary: Document title heading; not substantive regulatory content.

sections.section: PACKAGING AND TRANSPORT OF FRESH FRUIT AND VEGETABLES (CXC 44-1995)
sections.summary: Document title heading identifying the Code of Practice for packaging and transport of fresh fruit and vegetables (CXC 44-1995); not substantive on its own.

sections.section: SECTION I
sections.summary: Section heading only; not substantive regulatory content.

sections.section: 2.13 Produce requiring refrigeration should be thoroughly precooled, if necessary, prior to loading into transportation  equipment .  Produce  temperatures  should  be  taken  with  an  appropriate  thermometer  and recorded  on  the  bill  of  lading  for  future  reference.  The  load  compartment  in  the  equipment  also  should  be precooled to the recommended transport or storage temperature for the produce. It is advisable that the loading area should be enclosed and if available, the loading dock doorway area should be equipped with doorway air seals.
sections.summary: Requires produce that needs refrigeration to be thoroughly precooled before loading, with produce temperatures measured by an appropriate thermometer and recorded on the bill of lading, the load compartment precooled to recommended temperature, and advises enclosed loading areas and doorway air seals where available.

sections.section: 2.14 Proper loading practices are critical to maintaining temperature and relative humidity, protecting the produce from impact and vibration forces in transit, and preventing insects from entering the load . Special care must be taken when shipping mixed loads. The produce must be compatible.
sections.summary: States that proper loading practices are critical to maintain temperature and relative humidity, protect produce from impact and vibration, and prevent insect entry, and warns that mixed loads require special care and compatibility among produce items.

Lists basic loading methods for produce transport: bulk loading of unpackaged commodities, hand loading of individual shipping containers (with or without pallets), and unit loading of palletized or slipsheet container loads using pallet jacks or forklifts.

Warns that inadequate air circulation will ruin a load and advises keeping shipping containers off shallow floors and away from flat sidewalls using pallets, racks, and dunnage, allowing adequate headspace and room for air circulation under, around and through the load to protect produce from external heat gain, respiration heat, ethylene accumulation, heat loss in cold weather, and chilling or freezing during refrigeration.

sections.section:
2.17 Shippers using refrigerated transport equipment should follow the carrier's recommendations on loading of the equipment's load compartment to avoid chilling injury or freezing injury to the produce . Discharge air may be colder than the set-point temperature if the refrigeration system operates on return air

temperature sensing.
sections.summary: Advises shippers using refrigerated transport to follow carrier loading recommendations to avoid chilling or freezing injury and notes discharge air can be colder than the set-point if the refrigeration system senses return air temperature.

Specifies that loads should be secured to prevent vibration and impact damage using materials such as aluminum or wood load locks, paperboard or fibreboard honeycomb fillers, wood blocking and nailing strips, inflatable kraft paper air bags, cargo nets and straps, or wood load gates.

sections.section: 3.3 Bins,  boxes,  crates,  trays,  lugs,  baskets,  and  bags  are  considered  shipping  containers
sections.summary: Defines shipping containers (bins, boxes, crates, trays, lugs, baskets, bags), describes common fibreboard box styles and handling limitations (baskets hard to mix-load, bags provide limited protection), and requires fibreboard boxes used for wet or iced produce to be wax-impregnated or water-resistant with water-resistant adhesive, while warning that incorrect stacking orientation or misalignment can drastically reduce compression strength.

sections.section: 3.8 Wood pallets must be strong enough to allow storage under load . Provisions for forklift and pallet jack handling are necessary. The design of the bottom of the pallet should not block air circulation.
sections.summary: Requires wood pallets to be strong enough for storage under load and permit forklift/pallet jack handling without blocking air circulation, specifies adequate top deck boards or a ventilated fibreboard sheet to support boxes, forbids box overhang (which reduces strength), and advises cross-stacking or securing top layers of pallet loads for stability while cautioning that film wrap should not be used on ventilated produce containers.

sections.section: 3.8.1  Pallets must have an adequate number of top deck boards to support fibreboard boxes. Otherwise the boxes may collapse between deck boards from the overhead weight of the other containers, crush the produce, and cause the entire load to lean or fall off the pallet. A sheet of fibreboard with holes for air circulation can be used to distribute air across the pallet.
sections.summary: Requires pallets to have an adequate number of top deck boards to support fibreboard boxes to prevent collapse, crushing of produce, and load instability, and allows a perforated fibreboard sheet to be used to distribute air across the pallet.

sections.section: 3.8.2  Boxes must not overhang the edges of the pallets. Overhang can reduce the strength of fibreboard boxes by one-third. This condition also can lead to collapse of the entire load, crushing of the produce, and make loading, unloading, and storage in racks difficult. On the other hand, boxes which utilize less than 90% of the pallet surface and do not align with the pallet edge can shift in transit.
sections.summary: Prohibits boxes from overhanging pallet edges because overhang can reduce fibreboard box strength by about one-third and cause load collapse, produce crushing, and handling difficulties, and notes boxes using less than 90% of pallet surface or not aligned may shift in transit.

sections.section: 3.8.3  Pallet loads of shipping containers which are not strapped or netted should have at least the top three layers of containers cross-stacked to provide stability. Some shippers use film wrap, tape, or glue on the top layers  in  addition  to  cross-stacking.  The  containers  must  be  strong  enough  to  be  cross-stacked  without collapsing. Film wrap should not be used on shipping containers of produce that need ventilation.
sections.summary: Requires pallet loads that are not strapped or netted to have at least the top three layers cross-stacked for stability, specifies containers must be strong enough for cross-stacking, notes some shippers add film wrap/tape/glue, and warns film wrap should not be used on containers that require ventilation.

sections.section: 3.9 Slipsheets are used by some shippers because they cost less than pallets . They also eliminate the cost of  transporting and returning pallets. A special forklift is needed to transfer slipsheet loads  to and from the pallets  at  the  shipper's  and  receiver's  distribution  centre.  If  a  receiver  does  not  have  the  proper  handling equipment, the packages are unloaded by hand onto pallets for placement in storage. Shipping containers on slipsheets are cross-stacked, film wrapped, or otherwise unitized with cornerboards and strapping.
sections.summary: Describes slipsheets as a lower-cost alternative to pallets that require special forklifts at shipper/receiver, may necessitate manual unloading if receiver lacks equipment, and states slipsheeted shipping containers should be unitized (cross-stacked, film wrapped, or secured with cornerboards and strapping).

sections.section: 3.9.1  Slipsheets made of fibreboard or plastic must be strong enough to be clamped and pulled onto the forklift tines or plate for lifting while fully loaded. Fibreboard slipsheets should be wax impregnated when used in wet conditions. Slipsheets used in transportation equipment should have holes for air circulation under the load. The use of slipsheets in refrigerated transportation equipment with shallow floor channels is not recommended due to the need for adequate air circulation under the load.
sections.summary: Specifies slipsheets must be strong enough to be clamped and pulled when fully loaded, fibreboard slipsheets should be wax-impregnated in wet conditions, slipsheets used in transport should have holes for air circulation, and their use is not recommended in refrigerated equipment with shallow floor channels.

sections.section: ## - SECTION IV -
sections.summary: Section heading only: no substantive provisions in this entry.

sections.section: ## PRECOOLING PRACTICES
sections.summary: Introduces precooling practices recommending removal of field heat by precooling to recommended storage temperature and relative humidity as soon as possible after harvest to maintain fruit and vegetable quality, and outlines related handling recommendations (e.g., harvest timing, protecting produce from sun); it also notes refrigerated transport should not be used to remove field heat.

sections.section: 4.1 If  available,  the  removal  of field  heat  by  the  process  of  precooling  to  a  recommended  storage temperature and relative humidity is suggested to maintain the quality of fruits, and vegetables. The quality of most produce will rapidly deteriorate if field heat is not removed before loading into transportation equipment.
sections.summary: Recommends removing field heat by precooling produce to a recommended storage temperature and relative humidity to maintain quality and warns most produce will rapidly deteriorate if field heat is not removed before loading into transport.

sections.section: 4.2 Refrigerated transportation equipment is designed to  maintain temperature and should not be used to remove field heat from produce packed in shipping containers. The refrigeration units also are not capable of raising or controlling the relative humidity.
sections.summary: States refrigerated transportation equipment is intended to maintain temperature and should not be used to remove field heat from packed produce, and that such units cannot raise or control relative humidity.

sections.section: 4.3 Precooling extends produce life by reducing:
sections.summary: Lists that precooling extends produce life by reducing field heat, respiration and heat generated by produce, rate of ripening, moisture loss (shrivelling/wilting), ethylene production, and the spread of decay.

sections.section: 4.3.1  field  heat;
sections.summary: Identifies reduction of field heat as one of the benefits of precooling.

sections.section: 4.3.2  the  rate  of  respiration  and  heat generated by the produce;
sections.summary: Identifies reduction of the rate of respiration and heat generated by produce as a benefit of precooling.

sections.section: 4.3.3  the  rate  of  ripening;
sections.summary: Identifies reduction of the rate of ripening as a benefit of precooling.

sections.section: 4.3.4  the  loss  of  moisture (shrivelling and wilting);
sections.summary: Identifies reduction of moisture loss (shrivelling and wilting) as a benefit of precooling.

sections.section: 4.3.5  the  production  of  ethylene (ripening gas generated by the produce);
sections.summary: Identifies reduction of ethylene production (a ripening gas) as a benefit of precooling.

sections.section: 4.3.6  the  spread  of  decay.
sections.summary: Identifies reduction of the spread of decay as a benefit of precooling.

sections.section: 4.4 The success of precooling is dependent on:
sections.summary: States precooling success depends on factors including time between harvest and precooling, shipping container type, initial produce temperature, cold-air/water/ice flow, final produce temperature, sanitation of precooling media, and maintaining recommended temperature after precooling.

sections.section: 4.4.1  time  between  harvest  and  precooling;
sections.summary: Specifies that the time between harvest and precooling is a key factor affecting the success of precooling.

sections.section: 4.4.2  type  of  shipping  container  if  produce  is  packed beforehand;
sections.summary: Specifies that the type of shipping container used when produce is packed beforehand affects the success of precooling.

sections.section: 4.4.3  initial  produce  temperature;
sections.summary: Specifies that the initial produce temperature is a factor determining the effectiveness of precooling.
